Equity Research

Structured thematic equity research — rubric, valuation models, and adversarial review, packaged for offline reading.

Digital PDF: use bookmarks or tap a line above. Interactive HTML keeps filters and live charts.

Equity Research

Structured thematic equity research — rubric, valuation models, and adversarial review. Same scope as the PDF cover: from universe screen through valuation and decide.

What this pack covers. The dashboard scores a growth-oriented universe on a consistent rubric, runs multiple valuation models, stress-tests each thesis, and surfaces names for deeper work. Sections follow one pipeline: Discover (goals and weights), Screen (universe and shortlist), Decide (models + historic risk in one table), then Stock deep dive in the live HTML (charts and full narrative). The PDF adds Appendix B — one research page per name (charts, watchlist, triggers — screen-only in the PDF export).

How names are scored. Each company is scored 1–5 on six dimensions — growth, margins, balance sheet, durability, tail risks (higher is worse), and valuation (higher means cheaper). Scores combine into a composite out of 30 with equal weights and transparent qualitative criteria — no hidden weighting or black-box scoring.

Evidence and models. Fundamentals and history come primarily from yfinance (verify material numbers in SEC filings and company releases). Valuation layers include forward-multiple scenarios, DCF sensitivity grids, historical risk metrics, and Monte Carlo paths. The live HTML adds filters, sorting, TradingView charts, and directional social inputs from Reddit and X — not trading signals. AI assists theme research, earnings cross-checks, adversarial review, and sentiment scanning; every buy/sell/hold decision stays with you.

Using this PDF vs. the dashboard. The export is an offline reading pack (conclusions, evidence, model outputs). Expandable sections are inlined for print — layout is tuned for paper, not pixel-perfect parity with the screen. Treat cross-model disagreement as a filings and guidance checklist, not noise.

Report generated 12 May 2026. Underlying figures as of 2026-05-11 UTC unless noted in-body. This material is education and research, not personalised investment advice. yfinance fields can be delayed or mis-mapped; verify in filings before acting.

Disclaimer: No recommendation to buy or sell. No guarantee of returns. UK and other readers must confirm broker, ISA or SIPP eligibility, and tax treatment before trading.

Executive Summary

This report’s 55-name core shortlist (as of 2026-05-28) is drawn from a 489-company thematic universe. 7 names sit in Tier 1 (top composite-rank quartile within this shortlist: MU, B, FSLR, PSX, MPC, VLO (+1 more)). Selection follows the five-signal composite—scenario, rubric, risk, Monte Carlo, and DCF—with theme caps; conviction tiers use that composite rank, not the rubric scorecard alone.

Versus the prior snapshot: 4 added (CI, NDX1.DE, REGN, WDAY), 4 dropped (CNC, QRVO, TENB, VST), 21 unchanged. Sleeve emphasis: Quantum / frontier compute (20), AI infra & compute (13), Energy transition & grids (8).

Bottom line: Before overriding the composite screen, check theme-cap casualties and high-growth names still outside the pool — then confirm each hold in filings and adversarial packs (a gate, not a buy signal).

As of 2026-05-28 · 55-name core shortlist · 489 universe · 7 themes · ~355 modelled

Supporting view and watchouts

Why this shortlist

  • Revenue momentum on the shortlist: QUBT (+9364% YoY) · ASTS (+1952% YoY) · IONQ (+755% YoY).
  • Sleeve mix on the 55-name shortlist: Quantum / frontier compute (20), AI infra & compute (13), Energy transition & grids (8), Industrial automation & robotics (6), Health Tech (4).
  • Universe screen: 489 companies across 7 themes on US, UK, and European listings — rubric scored on latest quarterly earnings.
  • Selection uses the five-signal composite with theme caps — rubric rank alone does not add or drop names.
  • Tier 1 = top quartile on five-signal composite rank within this shortlist (not rubric ≥ 18).

Biggest risks

  • Tier 4 (lowest composite quartile on this shortlist): CRUS, DIOD, NVDA, PLAB, QUBT, TSM — may include theme-seat or cap-driven names; confirm thesis in filings.
  • Elevated drawdown in the lookback window: ASTS, AVIO.MI, BKSY, CRUS, DIOD — volatility is not thesis validation.
  • Quantum sleeve names remain narrative-heavy with extreme valuation risk — treat as long-dated optionality, not core compounders.
  • Key kill triggers in focus: ASTS: Two consecutive quarters of cash burn acceleration without funded runway through 2027 · Material gui; AVGO: Material guidance cut vs consensus on revenue or margins; AVIO.MI: Two consecutive quarters of cash burn acceleration without funded runway through 2027 · Material gui.

Discover

Investor goals, risk appetite, and the investment universe

You are building a growth portfolio across 7 structural themes, spanning US, UK, and European markets. Each theme gets a policy target weight (see theme_target_weights.json) used for Decide theme caps and the Shortlist ~Alloc % column — so the research queue tracks the same sleeve mix, renormalised across names that actually clear the bar. The approach is to drip-buy gradually — many small positions over time rather than deploying all at once. The universe includes NYSE, NASDAQ, London Stock Exchange, Euronext, and Xetra-listed names — all accessible to UK-based investors through major brokers. The table below shows policy weights; empty sleeves on Decide are called out under the Shortlist table after each refresh.

Target theme weights

ThemeIn simple wordsTarget %
AI infrastructure & computeChips, servers, data centres, and gear that run AI27%
Energy transition & gridsPower, grids, and equipment as electricity demand grows26%
Health TechTech-driven healthcare: robotic surgery, AI diagnostics, biotech pipelines14%
Industrial automation & roboticsFactories and robots doing more of the work11%
Cybersecurity & digital trustBlocking hacks and keeping data and systems safe9%
Fintech & digital moneyPayments, neobanks, exchanges, and financial infrastructure software8%
Quantum / frontier computeVery early "next" computers—not yet a big business for most names5%
Total100%

Universe coverage: 489 companies across 7 themes on US, UK, and European exchanges, screened for direct thematic linkage to the demand driver — not just "AI" in the pitch deck. The manifest was enlarged so the rubric grid covers the same broad sleeve you screen before cutting to Decide.

Shortlist bar: Treat the Decide shortlist as provisional. If it still carries Tier 3 names or Tier 2 names at the softer end of the models, keep re-screening the full 7-theme universe (sort, stress-test, adversarial pass) until you surface a tighter set of tickers that clears the same evidence bar — do not treat the shortlist as finished just because it fits on one page.

Industry Trends

The macro, sector, and thematic forces shaping where opportunities exist

Three forces drive every theme below: the AI capital expenditure cycle pulling demand for chips, power, and security; grid modernisation and electrification reshaping energy markets; and regulatory tailwinds (IRA, CHIPS Act, NIST cybersecurity frameworks) creating policy floors under spending. Each theme card explains the demand drivers, where value accrues, and common mistakes to avoid.

Industry & macro narrative

Theme Thesis in one breath Where value tends to accrue Common mistake
AI infra (30%) Training, inference, and enterprise copilots need accelerators, HBM, networking, cooling, and power inside hyperscale data centres; cloud capex is the demand flywheel. GPUs/accelerators, memory, EDA, WFE (deposition/etch/metrology), DC networking, thermal/power subsystems — each layer has different cyclicality and customer concentration. Labelling any software “AI” without revenue tied to physical or design infrastructure that enables compute.
Energy & grids (28%) AI load plus electrification raises baseload and peak needs; regulated wires, merchant IPPs, renewables, and grid hardware all play different roles in the same macro story. Allowed ROE utilities, contracted PPAs, grid equipment backlogs, select solar/midstream — map who earns regulated vs merchant vs manufacturing margin. Equating long-run load growth with next-quarter power prices (weather, gas, policy noise).
Cyber (10%) More cloud + AI surface area raises breach risk; boards standardise on platforms, identity, and OT protection — renewals and module attach drive durable spend. Consolidating platforms, network appliances with installed base, SASE/identity specialists — compare net retention and module cross-sell, not logo counts. Treating all “cyber” as one factor; growth and retention dispersion across peers is wide.
Industrial auto (12%) Labour shortages, reshoring, and precision requirements push capex into robots, motion, sensors, and industrial software — but cycles bite when factories pause spend. Factory automation vendors, power management for plants and DCs, integrators with recurring service — check backlog quality vs one-off equipment. Confusing strong order headlines with durable profit when customers can cancel or defer capex quickly.
Health Tech (15%) Technology shifts outcomes (GLP-1, robotics, devices, targeted oncology); payers and patent clocks determine economics as much as clinical efficacy. Transformative franchises with runway, robotic platforms with consumables, select devices — diligence payer coverage and patent cliffs. Ignoring who pays; great science can still fail commercially if reimbursement tightens.
Fintech & digital money (8%) Payments rails, neobanks, exchanges, and financial market infrastructure — revenue tied to moving money, underwriting, or selling software to banks and merchants. Scaled payment networks and processors; neobanks with improving unit economics; exchanges, clearing, and market-data vendors with subscription or volume mix. Labelling any consumer app with a wallet as fintech without checking whether payments are material to revenue and margin.
Quantum (5%) Early-stage hardware, cryogenics, and cloud access bets; useful commercial advantage vs classical compute is still uneven; dilution and milestone risk dominate. Hardware paths, control electronics, enterprise hybrids (e.g. IBM) — read cash runway and revenue quality in filings. Confusing lab milestones with near-term per-share cash flows.
Space & defence Cross-cutting sleeve: launch cadence, satellite services, defence primes — long programmes, political risk, dilution for growth-stage space. Backlog visibility, services mix, execution on programmes vs hardware one-offs. Ignoring dilution and programme slips while focusing only on headline contract wins.
AI infrastructure & compute 27%

Chips, servers, data centres, and the gear that runs AI.

AI workloads — training large models, running inference at scale, and deploying enterprise copilots — need specialised hardware at every layer. That starts with GPUs and accelerators (the processors designed for parallel computation), but extends to high-bandwidth memory (the fast storage chips bolted next to processors), networking switches (the gear that connects thousands of chips inside a data centre), cooling systems (liquid and air solutions to remove heat from dense racks), and power delivery equipment. Big cloud companies like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google publish multi-year capital spending plans that run into tens of billions of dollars per quarter. That money flows down through a supply chain of chip designers, wafer equipment makers, packaging specialists, and infrastructure vendors — many of which are less well-known than the headline platform stocks.

  • What drives demand: Training and running AI models at scale; enterprise adoption of copilots and agents; sovereign AI programs where governments want domestic compute capacity; data-centre buildout for cloud workloads beyond AI.
  • Where value accrues: GPU and accelerator designers (NVDA, AMD); memory makers (MU); chip-design software or EDA tools (SNPS); wafer fabrication equipment or WFE — the machines that manufacture chips (LRCX, AMAT, KLAC); data-centre networking (ANET); power and cooling (VRT). Each layer has different margins, competition, and cyclicality.
  • How to learn: Read the "segment revenue" and "customer concentration" sections in 10-K filings. A company might report headline growth, but if 60% of revenue comes from two hyperscaler customers, that concentration is a risk. Also compare capex plans from cloud companies quarter to quarter — they signal future demand for the supply chain.
  • Common mistake: Treating every software company with "AI" in its pitch as an AI infrastructure play. The real test is whether the company's revenue comes from selling the physical or design tools that make AI work, or whether it is just adding AI features to an existing product.
NVDAAMDAVGOMUANETVRTLRCXAMATKLACSNPS IFX.DE ASM.AS BESI.AS SOITEC.PA AIXA.DE VAT.SW NOD.OL MEL.BR XFAB.PA STM
Energy transition & grids 26%

Power plants, grids, and equipment that keep the lights on as electricity demand rises.

AI is not only a software story — it is increasingly a physics and infrastructure story. A single large data centre can draw as much power as a small city. That means more baseload generation (gas, nuclear, or renewables running around the clock), more peak capacity, upgrades to transmission lines and substations, and investment in energy storage. 'Energy transition' here covers the whole system: from the power plant to the socket, including the companies that build, maintain, and operate it. Regulated utilities (whose returns are set by regulators) sit alongside merchant power producers (whose profits depend on market electricity prices) and renewable developers — each with different risk profiles.

  • What drives demand: Electrification of transport and industry; data-centre load growth (estimated to double or triple in many grids by 2030); grid modernisation to handle variable renewables; government policy incentives for clean energy.
  • Where value accrues: Regulated utilities earn returns set by their regulator — slower but steadier (NEE, XEL, WEC, SO, DUK, NGG, SSE.L). Independent power producers or IPPs earn market prices — more volatile (VST, CEG). Solar module manufacturers (FSLR) and inverter makers (ENPH) depend on project pipelines and policy. Midstream pipeline operators (ET) move gas and liquids. Grid equipment companies sell transformers, switchgear, and HVDC links — often with multi-year backlogs (ENR.DE). Small modular nuclear reactors represent a new power source for data centres (RR.L).
  • How to learn: Read utility IRPs (integrated resource plans) — these are public documents showing how a utility plans to meet load over the next 10–20 years. Check interconnection queues (how many new projects are waiting to connect to the grid). Compare company commentary on data-centre contracts versus merchant exposure. When nuclear headlines or video coverage spike (SMR, baseload, data-centre power deals), extract tickers only as a watch list, then verify each against IRPs, licence milestones, and revenue mix the same way you would for any IPP or utility.
  • Common mistake: Confusing long-run load growth (a structural trend that takes years to play out) with short-run power prices (which can spike or crash because of weather, gas prices, or policy changes unrelated to your thesis).
VSTNEEXELWECETSODUKCEGENPHFSLRRR.LNGGSSE.LENR.DE ORSTED.CO IBE.MC VWS.CO RWE.DE ENEL.MI ENGI.PA CNA.L EDP.LS EDPR.LS NDX1.DE PRY.MI LR.PA NEX.PA DRX.L NEOEN.PA
Cybersecurity & digital trust 9%

Software and services that stop hacks and protect data.

More cloud workloads and AI systems mean a wider attack surface — more endpoints, more APIs, more data moving between services. At the same time, regulators and boards are raising the bar on security baselines. That makes parts of cyber spending behave like recurring infrastructure: companies renew security subscriptions the way they pay for electricity. The sector includes endpoint protection (software on laptops and servers), cloud workload security (protecting apps running in the cloud), network detection, identity management (controlling who can access what), and vulnerability scanning. Revenue is typically subscription-based, so the key metrics are renewal rates and net revenue retention — how much existing customers spend over time.

  • What drives demand: Headline-grabbing breaches and ransomware attacks that remind boards to spend; zero-trust architecture adoption (the idea that no user or device is trusted by default); identity for humans and machines; OT security for factories and utilities that are increasingly connected.
  • Where value accrues: Platform vendors trying to consolidate multiple security tools into one stack (PANW, CRWD); network security appliance makers with installed bases (FTNT, CHKP); zero-trust and SASE specialists (ZS); identity providers (OKTA); niche vulnerability and exposure management (QLYS, TENB). Each has different margin structures and growth rates.
  • How to learn: Compare ARR growth, net retention rate, and free cash flow margin across peers. Check whether a vendor is gaining "module attach" — selling additional products to existing customers. Read quarterly earnings for billings vs revenue cadence (billings often lead revenue by a quarter or two).
  • Common mistake: Assuming all cyber stocks move together. Net retention and module adoption differ sharply by vendor — one company growing 30% can sit next to a peer growing 10% in the same "cyber" bucket.
PANWCRWDFTNTZSOKTAGENCHKPQLYSTENBS NCC.L WS1V.HE YSN.DE
Industrial automation & robotics 11%

Factories and robots getting more automated.

Labour shortages, rising quality standards, and the reshoring of manufacturing all push factories toward more automation. This theme covers the full stack: physical robots that weld, assemble, and pack; motion control systems (motors, drives, actuators); sensors that give machines eyes and touch; industrial software that designs, simulates, and monitors production lines; and the integrators that tie everything together. The spending is cyclical — it rises when companies invest in new capacity and falls during downturns — so understanding order books and backlog quality matters as much as headline revenue growth.

  • What drives demand: Capital expenditure cycles in automotive, logistics, and electronics manufacturing; nearshoring (moving production closer to end markets); safety and precision requirements in food, pharma, and semiconductor fabs; labour shortages in ageing economies.
  • Where value accrues: Factory automation and software (ROK, SIE.DE); electrical power management for data centres and factories (ETN, SU.PA); robotics and electrification (ABB); diversified industrials with automation segments (HON, EMR); heavy machinery with services attach (CAT, DE); motion control and hydraulics (PH); climate and HVAC systems (TT, IR, ITW). Recurring services revenue is more durable than one-time equipment sales.
  • How to learn: Track book-to-bill ratios and order backlogs in quarterly filings. Check how much of revenue is recurring services versus one-time equipment. Look for "automation" or "digital" segments broken out in financial statements.
  • Common mistake: Confusing order growth with durable profit. Large backlogs can reverse quickly when customers cut capex in a downturn. Also, many "automation" conglomerates have large legacy segments unrelated to the theme — always check what percentage of revenue is actually automation.
ROKETNHONCATDEEMRPHIRITWTTSU.PASIE.DEABB ATCO-A.ST HEXA-B.ST DSY.PA RSW.L IMI.L SPX.L HLMA.L SXS.L KNEBV.HE ALFA.ST SAND.ST EPIR.ST KION.DE GEA.DE SMIN.L BA.L RHM.DE AIR.PA HO.PA SAF.PA LDO.MI
Health Tech 14%

Tech-driven healthcare — robotic surgery, AI diagnostics, biotech pipelines, structural heart devices.

This is not broad healthcare. It is narrowly focused on companies where technology creates a step-change in patient outcomes or hospital efficiency — and where that technology edge drives share-price upside. Robotic surgery systems generate high switching costs because surgeons train on specific platforms and hospitals invest in infrastructure around them. Biotech pipelines with transformative drugs (GLP-1 agonists for obesity, gene therapies, next-gen oncology) can create explosive revenue growth when they hit commercial inflection. AI-assisted diagnostics and structural heart devices represent smaller but growing niches. The common thread: each company is selling technology that is hard to substitute, not a commodity device or managed care plan.

  • What drives demand: Ageing populations worldwide; the obesity and cardiometabolic disease epidemic driving GLP-1 drug adoption; precision medicine making treatments more targeted; hospital pressure to improve efficiency and outcomes with technology.
  • Where value accrues: Pharma companies with transformative pipelines and patent runway (LLY, NVO); robotic surgery platforms with installed bases and recurring consumables (ISRG); biotech with concentrated but high-value franchises (VRTX); next-gen cardiac and structural heart devices gaining share (BSX); diversified pharma with oncology and vaccine franchises (AZN, GSK).
  • How to learn: For pharma, read trial readouts and payer commentary (who covers the drug and at what price). For devices, track procedure volumes and competitive dynamics quarter to quarter. For all, check patent expiry dates — they determine how long the revenue stream is protected.
  • Common mistake: Ignoring payers. A drug can be clinically excellent but economically challenged if governments or insurers push back on pricing. Who pays (government, insurer, out-of-pocket) determines economic value as much as clinical efficacy.
LLYISRGVRTXBSXNVOAZNGSK ROG.SW NOVN.SW SAN.PA SN.L LONN.SW AFX.DE STMN.SW COLO-B.CO DEMANT.CO SRT3.DE HIK.L UCB.BR GMAB.CO BANB.SW ARGX.BR
Fintech & digital money 8%

Payments rails, digital banking, trading platforms, and financial market infrastructure.

This sleeve covers companies whose revenue is tied to moving money, underwriting risk, or selling software to banks and merchants — not broad “tech” stories with a payments sidecar. Neobanks and BNPL names compete on unit economics and funding costs; exchanges and market-data vendors earn through volume and subscription mix; legacy processors and card networks scale on transaction counts. Banks and conglomerates appear here when the manifest tags them for payments, wealth, or market-structure exposure — always read the linkage line before treating them as pure fintech.

  • What drives demand: Digital wallet adoption; cross-border remittance; merchant acquiring growth; rate cycles affecting NIM and cash balances; regulatory clarity on crypto and BNPL.
  • Where value accrues: Scaled payment networks and processors (PYPL, FI); high-growth neobanks with improving credit (NU, SOFI); exchanges and clearing (ICE, CME); market data and ratings (SPGI, MCO, MSCI).
  • How to learn: Separate transaction growth from take-rate and credit losses. For banks, track NII vs fee income. For processors, read gross vs net revenue disclosure.
  • Common mistake: Labelling any consumer app with a wallet as fintech without checking whether payments are material to revenue and margin.
PYPLNUHOODSOFIINTUICEADYENWISE
Quantum / frontier compute 5%

Very early-stage quantum and next-gen compute bets.

Quantum computing aims to solve specific classes of problems — molecular simulation, optimisation, cryptography — by harnessing quantum mechanics. But useful commercial scale (where a quantum computer reliably solves a real business problem faster and cheaper than a classical computer) remains uneven. Most pure-play quantum stocks are pre-revenue or minimal-revenue, with high cash burn, frequent equity raises (dilution), and valuations driven more by narrative than fundamentals. The sleeve's 10% weight treats this as long-dated optionality: you are buying a lottery ticket on a technology that could be transformative, with the understanding that most of these companies may not survive in their current form. Adjacent 'frontier compute' includes the control electronics, cryogenics systems, and software stacks that quantum hardware needs — each with different risk profiles.

  • What drives interest: National competitiveness (governments funding quantum programs); the coming need to migrate encryption to post-quantum standards (NIST PQC timelines); partnerships between quantum startups and major cloud providers offering 'quantum as a service.'
  • Where value may accrue (eventually): Quantum hardware (trapped-ion, superconducting, photonic — competing approaches with no clear winner yet); control and cryogenic systems; error-correction and compiler software; cloud access platforms. IBM is the exception — quantum is a tiny fraction of a large services and mainframe business, giving it a different risk profile from pure plays.
  • How to learn: Read S-1 and 10-K risk factors carefully — they often explain the company's own view of how far away commercial viability is. Check cash runway (how many quarters until they need to raise again). Look at revenue quality: is it government grants, consulting, or actual product sales? When quantum is loud on YouTube or conference circuits, mine mentioned tickers only as hypotheses, then reconcile each name to the latest filings, funding rounds, and partnership disclosures before you add or swap a row in this universe.
  • Common mistake: Equating scientific milestones (a paper showing fewer errors, a new qubit record) with near-term per-share cash flows. A breakthrough in the lab does not mean revenue next quarter. Many names are dilution-heavy, meaning your ownership percentage shrinks with each equity raise.
IONQRGTIQBTSQUBTARQQLAESHOLOIBMAPLDFORM OXIG.L IQE.L
Cross-cutting: space & defence —

Launch providers, satellite operators, and defence primes — researched alongside automation and cyber themes.

Space and defence is not a separate line in your weight table, but it comes up frequently when researching automation, cyber, and energy themes. Launch providers like Rocket Lab (RKLB) are building vertical integration — manufacturing their own engines, spacecraft buses, and ground systems — which changes their margin profile over time. Satellite operators serve communications, earth observation, and government customers. Defence primes execute long multi-year programmes with predictable backlogs but can face cost overruns and political risk. The key learning checkpoints are backlog quality, programme execution, dilution (especially for growth-stage space companies that burn cash), and customer concentration (often a single government agency). When flagship launch or space-platform names are tied to very large capital raises or IPO rumours, sketch where cash is likely to land in the first 6–9 months (talent, suppliers, ground infrastructure, cadence ramp) and ask which listed ecosystem names already map to your weighted themes — that is how you judge whether to add or swap tickers versus the current shortlist.

  • What drives demand: Geopolitics and national security budgets; commercial launch demand for satellite constellations; earth observation and connectivity services; defence modernisation programmes.
  • Where value accrues: Launch providers that achieve reusability and high cadence; satellite bus and component manufacturers; defence primes with long-cycle programmes. Services (data, imagery, comms) can be higher-margin than hardware.
  • How to learn: Track launch cadence and backlog for launch providers. Read programme-level commentary in defence earnings calls. Check dilution history for growth-stage space companies — share count growth often outpaces revenue growth.
  • Common mistake: Ignoring programme delays and dilution in growth-stage space companies. A company can win contracts and still destroy shareholder value if it keeps issuing new shares to fund operations.
  • Suspected IPO / listing calendar (6–12 months): Track rumoured filings across space, power, and semis as a cap-ex, hiring, and supplier shock — not a price target. Map whether trickle-down spend overlaps the themes you already weight (AI infra, grids, cyber) and whether that implies new universe rows or shortlist swaps after the same rubric screen.
RKLB

Screen & Shortlist

What is this? A scorecard to compare companies side by side on the same six dimensions. Click any score column to sort highest-to-lowest (click again to reverse). Not a formula — just a structured way to keep your analysis consistent. Shortlist optimisation: whenever the Decide pack still shows Tier 3 or marginal Tier 2 names, treat the universe table below as an open search — keep sorting across all themes until you find tickers that beat the incumbents on the same rubric and model evidence, then refresh the shortlist.

Coverage: 489 names across 7 themes on US, UK, and European exchanges, scored against latest quarterly earnings data — not a recommendation list. Some structures (e.g. ET midstream MLPs) can be awkward in UK ISAs; confirm with your broker.

Growth, margins, balance sheet, durability: 1 = weak, 5 = strong. Tail risks: 1 = fewer headwinds, 5 = more headwinds. Valuation: higher = relatively cheaper / easier to justify, lower = pricier. — = not used. Illustration only—not advice and not one combined score.

How companies were selected

Each of the 489 companies in the manifest was scored using a structured one-pager process backed by latest quarterly earnings data:

  1. Universe construction: Up to 10+ names per theme across US, UK, and European exchanges, sourced from industry research and supply chain analysis. Each name must have a clear linkage to the theme's demand driver — not just "AI" in the pitch deck.
  2. Six-dimension rubric: Every company scored 1–5 on growth momentum, margin quality, balance-sheet strength, competitive durability, tail risks, and valuation. Each score requires a dated, citable evidence point.
  3. Proposed names: Selected by favouring companies with stronger balance-sheet and durability scores relative to their tail-risk exposure, then cross-checked against theme weight alignment. Higher tail-risk names stay on the watchlist until a full adversarial review is complete.
  4. Shortlist optimisation loop: The 25 core names in the Decide view were taken forward after screening the full 489-name manifest — a working set, not a trophy case. If any slot is Tier 3 or sits at the weaker end of Tier 2 after models reconcile, re-run the full universe screen (all 7 themes), promote any ticker that clears the bar more cleanly, and demote laggards — repeat until the shortlist is the best available fit from the grid, not the first 25 you stopped on.

The rubric is qualitative and transparent — no hidden weighting or black-box scoring. A "top pick" still requires a completed adversarial review regardless of rubric scores.

How companies were selected

Each of the 489 companies in the manifest was scored using a structured one-pager process backed by latest quarterly filings and earnings releases:

  1. Universe construction. Up to 10+ names per theme across US, UK, and European exchanges, sourced from industry research and supply-chain analysis. A name enters only with a clear line-of-sight to the theme’s demand driver — not a ticker label or marketing claim.
  2. Six-dimension rubric. Every row is scored 1–5 on growth momentum, margin quality, balance-sheet strength, competitive durability, tail risks, and valuation. Each score carries a dated, citable evidence point from the latest quarter; there is no undisclosed weighting or black-box scoring.
  3. Shortlist and proposed names. The shortlist elevates names that pair stronger balance-sheet and durability with controlled tail risk, checked against your theme weights. Higher tail-risk names remain on the watchlist until a full adversarial review is complete; a “top pick” still requires that pack regardless of rubric scores.
  4. Shortlist optimisation loop. The 25 Decide names are the core set taken forward after screening the full 489-name manifest — treat them as provisional. If any remain Tier 3 or weak Tier 2 after reconciliation, sweep the entire 7-theme universe again, promote stronger fits, and only then lock the list — optimisation beats stopping at the first pass.

The composite total is transparent: Growth + Margins + Balance sheet + Durability + Valuation − Tail risks (each dimension scored 1–5). Typical range about 0–24; higher is better if the dimensions are honest.

Dimension What the 1–5 scale captures
Growth Sales and demand trajectory — not a single quarter’s noise. 5 = a durable inflection supported by evidence in filings and guidance.
Margins How well revenue converts to profit — gross trajectory, operating leverage, and cash conversion. 5 = structural margin lift, not one-off cost cuts.
Balance sheet Debt, liquidity, and refinancing risk versus cash and earnings power. 5 = net cash or very low leverage with manageable maturities.
Durability Competitive moat — switching costs, scale, IP, or regulation. 5 = the edge is likely to survive aggressive competition.
Tail risks Low-probability, high-impact shocks (regulation, concentration, geopolitics, technology shifts). 5 = more serious tail risk — worse for the name. This dimension is inverted in the total.
Valuation Price versus the fundamental story and peers. 5 = easier to justify; cheap can still be a trap if the business is impaired.
Total Composite total: G + M + BS + D + V − Tail (each 1–5). Higher total = stronger profile with more tail risk subtracted.

What each score measures

Growth

Is revenue or the key metric trending up durably, or was it a one-off bump?

5 = strong, sustained inflection backed by evidence.

Margins

Gross margin trajectory, operating leverage, and how well revenue converts to free cash flow.

5 = expanding margins with a clear structural driver.

Balance sheet

How much debt the company carries relative to cash and earnings — and whether refinancing is a risk.

5 = net cash or very low leverage, no near-term maturities.

Durability

Does the company have a real competitive moat — switching costs, network effects, or regulatory barriers?

5 = deep lock-in; customers rarely leave.

Tail risks

Exposure to low-probability, high-impact events: regulation, customer concentration, geopolitics, or tech obsolescence.

5 = multiple serious tail risks. NOTE: higher is worse here.

Valuation

Is the current price reasonable given the growth outlook? Expensive can be fine; cheap can be a trap.

5 = clearly cheap relative to fundamentals and peers.

Total

G + M + BS + D + V − Tail. Each letter is a 1–5 score; tail risk is subtracted because higher tail = worse. Typical range about 0–24.

A high score means strong fundamentals with low risk exposure.

PDF rubric (shortlist only): Dimension definitions are in Screen & Shortlist → “How companies were selected”. Shading: Growth–Valuation = 1–5 (greener = stronger); Tail inverted (greener = lower risk). Total = tiered bands. One-line notes: green = strong YoY signal, red = clear decline language, grey = mixed/neutral.

Show rows:

Shortlist rubric: Only the Decide shortlist (25 names) — same set developed in Research and Value. Full universe stays in the HTML dashboard.

Theme Ticker Growth Margins Balance sheet Durability Tail risks Valuation Total One-line note
1–5 · sales / demand · 5 = durable inflection 1–5 · profit on sales · 5 = structural margin lift 1–5 · debt vs cash · 5 = net cash / low leverage 1–5 · moat · 5 = edge survives competition 1–5 · headwinds · 5 = more tail risk (worse) 1–5 · price vs story · 5 = easier to justify G+M+BS+D+V−Tail

Shortlist

Which names advance from screening to detailed analysis, based on rubric scores, theme linkage, and balance of growth vs risk. This is a work queue — not a basket, not position sizes, and not a recommendation to trade.

Shortlist changes (this refresh)

Shortlist = top ~60 by five-signal composite (scenario, rubric, risk, Monte Carlo, DCF percentiles; see shortlist_weights.json where composite mode applies), then theme caps to 20–28 names. Rubric alone does not decide adds or drops.

Not advice. ~Alloc % splits each theme's policy weight across Decide names in that sleeve (renormalised to 100% for the names on the list). Anything here needs your own filings pass, ISA/SIPP eligibility at your broker, and a full adversarial pack before real money.

Full thesis, market context, and kill criteria for each name are in Monitor (select ticker or use Deep dive links in the table).

Method: Names with stronger balance-sheet and durability scores relative to tail-risk exposure are prioritised. Quantum names remain "theme only" until a full adversarial review is complete. Research time is allocated in proportion to theme weights — more depth on AI and energy than quantum.

Action items

  1. Align any handwritten tiers or exec-summary bullets with the latest matrix rows.
  2. When valuation assumptions change materially, re-run the full pipeline before trusting scenario/Monte Carlo columns.

Appendix: AI Architecture and Data Sources

What powers this platform, where the data comes from, and how to reproduce the analysis

Data sources

yfinance (Python)

Fundamentals: price, market cap, P/E ratios, EPS, revenue, margins, cash flow, analyst targets. Historical price data for risk metrics and Monte Carlo inputs. Free, no API key required. Can be delayed or incorrect — always verify against filings.

TradingView (embedded widget)

Live candlestick charts for visual price action and timing assessment. Loaded client-side via TradingView's public widget API. Requires internet connection.

Reddit and X (directional only)

Finnhub free-tier context: analyst recommendation mix, insider MSPR, recent headline count, earnings timing. Not retail social sentiment.

SEC filings (manual reference)

10-K and 10-Q filings for earnings verification, risk factors, segment breakdowns, and management commentary. Not automatically pulled — used to cross-check yfinance data.

Screening methodology

Every company is scored 1–5 on six dimensions: growth (revenue trajectory), margins (gross and operating margin quality), balance sheet (debt vs cash), durability (competitive moat strength), tail risks (low-probability high-impact threats — higher is worse), and valuation (price relative to fundamentals — higher means cheaper). Scores are equal-weighted into a composite total out of 30. The rubric is qualitative and transparent — no hidden weighting or black-box scoring.

Valuation models

Forward-multiple scenario analysis

Takes current earnings, grows them at three rates (bull / base / bear) over 3 years, applies exit multiples, and probability-weights the results.

DCF sensitivity

Discounted cash flow grids testing 5 growth rates against 5 WACC assumptions. Each cell shows the implied share price and percentage difference from current market price.

Historical risk metrics

Two years of daily price data: beta (market sensitivity), annualised volatility, maximum drawdown, Sharpe ratio, and S&P 500 correlation.

Monte Carlo simulation

10,000 random price paths using geometric Brownian motion. Outputs probability of 50%+ gain and 30%+ loss over the projection period.

AI workflow

Claude supports each stage of the pipeline:

  • Theme research: AI identifies demand drivers, supply chain nodes, and overlooked names within each theme.
  • Earnings analysis: Latest quarterly data pulled via yfinance. AI cross-references against management commentary and flags thesis contradictions.
  • Adversarial review: AI generates structured bear-case bullets, stress points, and kill criteria for each proposed name.
  • Sentiment scanning: Directional social signals aggregated from Reddit and X.

Claude Code Skills

The AI-powered stages of this pipeline are driven by four specialised skills:

thematic-equity-research

The core skill. Defines the full research pipeline — company one-pagers, social sentiment scanning, and adversarial thesis review packs. Sets pillar taxonomy, operating principles (order matters, separate growth from valuation, evidence ladder, kill criteria), communication style rules, and UK-resident execution checklist.

web-scraping

Powers earnings data extraction via yfinance and social sentiment gathering from Reddit and X. Handles anti-bot patterns, content extraction cascades, and graceful degradation when APIs are unavailable.

investment-advisor

General investment analysis patterns applied to rubric scoring, valuation model selection, and portfolio construction logic. Provides frameworks for risk assessment and position sizing — not personalised advice.

mbb-consultant

Structured problem-solving and strategic frameworks (issue trees, MECE breakdowns, hypothesis-driven analysis) applied to thesis construction, industry analysis, and the adversarial review process.

Guardrails

  • Every page includes a disclaimer: this is education and research, not personalised investment advice.
  • No name advances to "proposed" status without a completed adversarial review.
  • All rubric scores require dated, citable evidence — no subjective scoring without data.
  • Human-in-the-loop: the platform surfaces analysis, but the investor makes every buy/sell/hold decision.
  • Data freshness: yfinance data can be delayed or incorrect. Always verify critical numbers in SEC filings before acting.

Appendix B — Company research

One page per name (composite shortlist, then personal holdings). Regenerated on refresh.

CRUS — Cirrus Logic, Inc. (composite shortlist)

At a glance. Tier 4 · Rubric 17/24 · Scenario wt +69% · MC median +61% · DCF mid +5%
